mappings.rationale: While focused on modern Japan, provides context for the Tokugawa period's transition to modernity including the bakuhan system's collapse and the opening of Japan to Western influences.
Directly relevant to the section's multiple references to literary developments across all periods of Japanese history, from the Nara period through the Tokugawa era.
Buddhism is a central theme throughout the section, from its introduction in the Yamato period through the growth of Zen in the Kamakura period and its cultural influence.
Relevant to the section's discussion of Neo-Confucianism in the Kamakura period and Confucianism's role in the Tokugawa era including the Shinto revival.
Directly relevant to the section's extensive coverage of the rise of the samurai class, the feudal bakufu system, and the samurai shugo as feudal lords.
Directly relevant to subsection D covering Korean history from prehistoric origins through the Three Kingdoms, Koryo dynasty, and Yi dynasty to 1910.
The section discusses archaeological sources for understanding prehistoric Japanese and Korean cultures, including Jomon and Yayoi pottery cultures.
Relevant to the section's coverage of Japanese imperial succession, the shogunate system, and Korean dynastic history including the Koryo and Yi dynasties.
Relevant to the section's discussion of resistance to Mongol invasions (1274 and 1281), the arrival of Portuguese firearms (1543), and military developments under the feudal system.
Relevant to the section's discussion of the Mongol invasions of Japan (1274 and 1281), the Mongol conquest of Korea (1231-1261), and the broader Mongol impact on East Asian geopolitics.
